content.text: Async subprocesses are a mechanism for launching and communicating with external OS-level processes — shell commands, scripts, or compiled binaries — from within an
program, without blocking the event loop while waiting for those processes to run or produce output.

, the calling thread blocks until the child process finishes. In an async program this is catastrophic — blocking the thread also blocks the event loop, meaning every other coroutine, network connection, and timer in the program stalls completely. Asyncio's answer is
, which wrap OS subprocess mechanics in async-friendly
interfaces, allowing concurrent subprocess management without any additional threads.
Many real programs need to shell out: running

, spawning a compiler or linter, or running user-submitted code in a sandboxed process. In a synchronous program each such call holds the CPU hostage for its duration. In an async server with dozens of concurrent requests, one 10-second
call would freeze every other pending connection.

Async subprocesses solve this by integrating with the event loop's I/O multiplexing. The event loop registers the subprocess's pipe file descriptors with the OS I/O notification mechanism (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS) so it receives a callback only when data arrives or the process exits — identical to how it handles network sockets. During the wait, the event loop is free to service any number of other coroutines.
(on Windows) to spawn a real OS process with its own PID and memory space. Three OS pipes are created and connected to the child's
file descriptors. These pipes are set to non-blocking mode.
Internally, asyncio wraps each pipe in a
. These are low-level abstractions in asyncio's framework. The transport handles raw byte movement; the protocol defines callbacks (
, etc.). For most practical work the high-level API is used exclusively and the transport/protocol layer is invisible.
— the same class used for TCP connections. Writing to it puts bytes into the pipe leading to the child's stdin.
that suspend the coroutine until data arrives, without blocking anything else.
suspends the coroutine until the child's exit code is available.
is a convenience that sends all input, reads all stdout/stderr, and closes stdin atomically.

The child process runs independently from the Python event loop. It has its own memory space and is scheduled by the OS. When it writes to its stdout, those bytes pass through the OS pipe buffer into the Python process's non-blocking file descriptor, triggering a read event that wakes up whatever coroutine is awaiting
The event loop spawns the OS child process and receives three non-blocking pipe ends. Each pipe is registered with the event loop's I/O notification system. Coroutines
on those stream objects; the event loop suspends them and resumes them only when data is ready or the process exits. The returned
object ties all three streams and the wait mechanism together.
Executes a program directly, bypassing the shell. Arguments are passed as separate strings, which avoids shell injection vulnerabilities and shell metacharacter interpretation.

import asyncio

async def run_direct():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "ls", "-la", "/tmp",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc.communicate()
    print(stdout.decode())
    print("Exit code:", proc.returncode)

asyncio.run(run_direct())
Passes the command string to the system shell (
on Unix). Enables shell features like pipes (
), and environment variable expansion. Introduces shell injection risk if any part of the command string is constructed from untrusted input.

async def run_shell():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
        "find /tmp -name '*.log' | wc -l",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    stdout, _ = await proc.communicate()
    print("Log count:", stdout.decode().strip())
with a list of arguments whenever the command does not need shell features. Use
Both functions share the same parameters as
Set StreamReader internal buffer size (default 64 KiB)
The simplest pattern — suitable when the process is short-lived and the full output fits in memory:

import asyncio

async def capture():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "python3", "-c", "print('hello from child')",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc.communicate()
    # proc.returncode is set after communicate() returns
    if proc.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Process failed: {stderr.decode()}")
    return stdout.decode().strip()

result = asyncio.run(capture())
print(result)  # "hello from child"

handles three things atomically: it sends any provided input data, reads stdout and stderr to EOF, and waits for the process to exit. It protects against a common deadlock (described in the mistakes section below).
Pattern 2: Streaming stdout line by line
When the process produces continuous output — a build log, a monitoring tool, real-time inference output — consuming one line at a time prevents buffering the entire output in memory:

import asyncio

async def stream_lines():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "ping", "-c", "5", "127.0.0.1",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL,
    )
    async for line in proc.stdout:
        # Each iteration suspends until a '\n' arrives
        print("Got line:", line.decode().rstrip())
    
    await proc.wait()
    print("Exit code:", proc.returncode)

asyncio.run(stream_lines())
under the hood and suspends the coroutine between iterations.
Pattern 3: Reading stdout and stderr concurrently

Reading from two pipes sequentially risks deadlock — the child could fill the stderr pipe buffer and block, while the Python side is blocked waiting on stdout. The solution is to read both pipes concurrently using

import asyncio

async def read_both():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "bash", "-c", "echo out1; echo err1 >&2; echo out2",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )

    async def read_stream(stream, label):
        async for line in stream:
            print(f"[{label}] {line.decode().rstrip()}")

    await asyncio.gather(
        read_stream(proc.stdout, "stdout"),
        read_stream(proc.stderr, "stderr"),
    )
    await proc.wait()

asyncio.run(read_both())
To send data into a running subprocess, write to
) and then drain before reading back:

import asyncio

async def interactive():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "cat",                          # 'cat' echoes everything back
        stdin=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    
    proc.stdin.write(b"hello asyncio\n")
    await proc.stdin.drain()            # flush the internal buffer
    
    proc.stdin.close()                  # send EOF
    await proc.stdin.wait_closed()      # wait for OS-level close
    
    output = await proc.stdout.read()
    print(output.decode())             # "hello asyncio\n"
    await proc.wait()

asyncio.run(interactive())
if the pipe could fill up. Always close stdin and wait for
before reading to EOF — some processes only exit after receiving EOF on stdin.
This is the core value proposition — running many processes simultaneously with a single thread:

import asyncio

async def run_one(cmd: list[str]) -> tuple[int, str]:
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        *cmd,
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.STDOUT,
    )
    stdout, _ = await proc.communicate()
    return proc.returncode, stdout.decode()

async def run_all():
    commands = [
        ["sleep", "2"],
        ["echo", "fast"],
        ["python3", "-c", "import time; time.sleep(1); print('done')"],
    ]
    # All three run in parallel; total time ≈ 2 seconds, not 3+
    results = await asyncio.gather(*(run_one(cmd) for cmd in commands))
    for code, out in results:
        print(f"exit={code} output={out.strip()!r}")

asyncio.run(run_all())
to impose a deadline. When the timeout elapses, the coroutine is cancelled and the process must be killed explicitly:

import asyncio

async def run_with_timeout():
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "sleep", "100",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    try:
        stdout, _ = await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=5.0)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        proc.kill()
        await proc.wait()        # reap the zombie; never skip this
        raise RuntimeError("Process timed out and was killed")
. Failing to reap the process leaves a zombie in the OS process table.
Wait for the process to exit
Real-world example: parallel code linting pipeline
An AI code review service needs to run
(security checker) against a submitted file, all in parallel, and collect their results within a deadline:

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class LintResult:
    tool: str
    returncode: int
    output: str

async def run_linter(tool: str, args: list[str], source_path: str) -> LintResult:
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        *args, source_path,
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.STDOUT,   # merge: linters mix both streams
    )
    try:
        stdout, _ = await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=30.0)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        proc.kill()
        await proc.wait()
        return LintResult(tool=tool, returncode=-1, output="timed out")
    return LintResult(tool=tool, returncode=proc.returncode, output=stdout.decode())

async def lint_file(path: str) -> list[LintResult]:
    tasks = [
        run_linter("ruff",   ["ruff", "check", "--output-format=json"], path),
        run_linter("mypy",   ["mypy", "--no-error-summary"],            path),
        run_linter("bandit", ["bandit", "-q", "-f", "json"],            path),
    ]
    # All three run concurrently; total time = max(individual times)
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    return [r for r in results if isinstance(r, LintResult)]

# Entry point
async def main():
    results = await lint_file("submission.py")
    for r in results:
        status = "✓" if r.returncode == 0 else "✗"
        print(f"{status} {r.tool} (exit {r.returncode})")

asyncio.run(main())
In production this pattern handles hundreds of concurrent submissions. Each
call costs one OS-level spawn per tool but zero threads. The event loop manages all pipe I/O and process wait notifications.

, the child process becomes a zombie (its exit status has not been collected). On Linux, zombie processes accumulate in the process table and can eventually exhaust the PID space or file descriptor limits. Always pair every

# Wrong
proc.kill()

# Correct
proc.kill()
await proc.wait()

OS pipes have a finite kernel buffer (typically 64 KiB). If a process writes more data than the buffer holds on one stream while the Python side is reading the other, the child blocks waiting for the buffer to drain, and the Python side waits for the child — a classic deadlock.

# Wrong — if stderr > 64 KiB, this deadlocks:
stdout_data = await proc.stdout.read()   # blocks until EOF
stderr_data = await proc.stderr.read()   # never reached

# Correct — read both concurrently:
stdout_data, stderr_data = await asyncio.gather(
    proc.stdout.read(),
    proc.stderr.read(),
)
await proc.wait()
handles this correctly internally — it uses
The whole purpose of async subprocesses is defeated if the code also uses synchronous subprocess calls:

# Wrong — blocks the event loop for the full duration:
import subprocess
result = subprocess.run(["ffmpeg", ...])

# Correct:
proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec("ffmpeg", ...)
Constructing a shell command string from user-supplied data is a critical vulnerability:

# Dangerous — user_input could be "; rm -rf /"
proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(f"grep {user_input} file.txt")

# Safe — each argument is passed directly, never interpreted by a shell:
proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec("grep", user_input, "file.txt")
If the protocol is interactive (write → read → write → read), forgetting
means the write stays buffered in Python's memory and the child process never receives it, causing the reader to block forever.
6. Assuming the process is still alive.
while the process runs. Check it before assuming success;
Many programs read from stdin until EOF and only then produce output. If
is never called, the child waits indefinitely for more input.
when the child process depends on relative paths. The default working directory inherits from the Python process, which can be surprising.
Copy the environment and extend it

import os
env = os.environ.copy()
env["MY_VAR"] = "value"
proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec("tool", env=env)
for streams the program doesn't need.
Attaching unused pipes wastes file descriptors and buffer memory.
Spawning thousands of processes simultaneously can exhaust file descriptors, memory, and CPU. Use
to cap the maximum concurrent processes:

sem = asyncio.Semaphore(10)   # at most 10 subprocesses at once

async def bounded_run(cmd):
    async with sem:
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(*cmd, ...)
        return await proc.communicate()
cancels all remaining tasks if one fails, which is usually the desired behavior for batch subprocess jobs.
gives the child process a chance to flush buffers and clean up temporary files. Only escalate to
if the process does not exit within a grace period.
call produces a real OS process. The event loop monitors all their pipe file descriptors via
— the same I/O poller used for network sockets. The overhead per subprocess is:
One OS fork/exec system call pair (~1–5 ms depending on process startup).
Three pipe pairs (six file descriptors) per process.
The Python side adds essentially no per-process CPU cost between launches and completions; the event loop simply waits for I/O events.
On Linux the default per-process FD limit is 1024 (

). Each subprocess consumes at least 3 FDs for pipes plus 1 for the process handle. At 1024 FDs that caps out at roughly 200 concurrent subprocesses before hitting EMFILE errors. In production, raise the limit (
) and use a semaphore to bound actual concurrency below that limit.
Process creation overhead dominates at small scale.
For tasks that take under ~100 ms, the fork/exec overhead is a meaningful fraction of total time. In such cases, consider whether a Python-native solution (using
for CPU-bound work, or in-process logic) would be faster than shelling out.

Each child process starts with a copy-on-write fork of the parent's virtual address space. In large Python programs (those that import heavy libraries like PyTorch or NumPy), this can mean each child temporarily maps hundreds of megabytes. On memory-constrained machines, spawn a dedicated lightweight launcher process rather than forking the full-weight Python process.
(which selects the Proactor automatically on Windows since Python 3.8).
are part of the standard library. No additional dependencies are required.
A compatibility layer that wraps both
provide the same subprocess capabilities with a cleaner API and richer timeout/cancellation semantics. Useful when writing library code that must work with either backend.
An alternative async framework with its own
that uses structured concurrency natively, making subprocess lifecycle management safer and less error-prone.
When the subprocess itself is tiny and the real bottleneck is a CPU-bound Python computation, offloading to a thread pool via
is sometimes simpler than the full async subprocess API.
A high-performance drop-in replacement for asyncio's default event loop (based on
). Subprocess I/O throughput improves substantially for pipe-heavy workloads.
Best for CPU-bound Python, not external programs
The single largest security risk is using
with unsanitized user input. Any shell metacharacter in the command string — semicolons, backticks,
, pipes — can execute arbitrary commands. The mitigation is always to use
with the command as a list.
A child that has exited but whose exit status has not been collected by
becomes a zombie. In long-running servers, accumulated zombies eventually exhaust the PID namespace. Always call
after every subprocess, even on the error path.
Uncontrolled subprocess spawning can exhaust file descriptors, process slots, memory, and CPU simultaneously. A
-based concurrency limit is mandatory in production.
Described in the mistakes section — always read stdout and stderr concurrently, or use

Running subprocesses as the same OS user as the Python process means a compromised command inherits that user's permissions. For user-submitted code execution, spawn child processes in a restricted environment: a separate OS user with minimal privileges, in a container or namespace, with resource limits set via
immediately terminates the child but may leave temporary files or locks uncleaned. Prefer
first and implement a graceful shutdown handler in the child if it manages state.
bytes if less data is currently available. For binary protocols with fixed-length messages, use
spawns real OS processes and wraps their pipes as
objects, integrating subprocess I/O into the event loop's non-blocking machinery.
for simple cases; read stdout and stderr concurrently (via
) for streaming or large-output cases to avoid pipe deadlock.
to reap the process, even after
to avoid shell injection; pass arguments as a list, not as a formatted string.
— unconstrained spawning exhausts file descriptors and memory.
On Windows, asyncio subprocess support requires
library provides a higher-level, framework-agnostic subprocess API worth preferring in reusable library code.
